section.title: Thanksgiving and Prayer
section.sectionRationale: Paul opens to a church he has never met, planted by Epaphras, and does two things before he argues anything: he gives thanks for their faith, love, and hope, and he prays that they would be filled with the knowledge of God's will. The greeting and thanksgiving already sound the letter's themes, the gospel bearing fruit worldwide, growth in knowledge, and rescue into the kingdom of God's beloved Son, so that the coming defense of Christ's supremacy rests on a foundation of gratitude and prayer.
section.thesis: Because the gospel has taken root in Colossae and is bearing fruit across the world, Paul thanks God for their faith, love, and hope, and prays they would be filled with the knowledge of God's will, walking worthy of the Lord who has rescued them from darkness into the kingdom of His beloved Son.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting from Paul and Timothy; thanksgiving for the Colossians' faith, love, and hope through the fruitful gospel; and the prayer that they be filled with knowledge and strengthened, giving thanks to the Father who rescued and redeemed them.

units.label: Greeting
units.note: grace and peace to the saints at Colossae

verses.text: Paul, an {{apostolos|apostle}} of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and Timothy our brother,
To the saints and faithful brothers in Christ at Colossae: Grace and peace to you from God our Father.

units.label: Thanksgiving for faith, love, and hope

verses.text: We always thank God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, when we pray for you,
because we have heard about your faith in Christ Jesus and your love for all the saints,
the faith and love proceeding from the hope stored up for you in heaven, of which you have already heard in the word of truth, the {{euangelion|gospel}}
that has come to you. All over the world this gospel is bearing fruit and growing, just as it has been doing among you since the day you heard it and truly understood the grace of God.
You learned it from Epaphras, our beloved fellow servant, who is a faithful minister of Christ on our behalf,
and who also informed us of your love in the Spirit.

units.label: The prayer and the rescue
units.note: filled with knowledge, brought into the kingdom of the Son

verses.text: For this reason, since the day we heard about you, we have not stopped praying for you and asking God to fill you with the {{epignosis|knowledge}} of His will in all spiritual wisdom and understanding,
so that you may walk in a manner worthy of the Lord and may please Him in every way: bearing fruit in every good work, growing in the knowledge of God,
being strengthened with all power according to His glorious might so that you may have full endurance and patience, and joyfully
giving thanks to the Father, who has qualified you to share in the inheritance of the saints in the light.
He has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of His beloved Son,
in whom we have {{apolutrosis|redemption}}, the forgiveness of sins.

apostolos.gloss: A sent one, an authorized envoy commissioned to represent the sender. Paul is an apostle of Christ Jesus not by his own ambition but by the will of God, which gives his words to this unvisited church their weight. The title grounds the whole letter: what follows is not private opinion but the message of the risen Christ through His appointed messenger.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, the announcement of a victory or royal accession. Paul calls it the word of truth, a living message that is bearing fruit and growing across the whole world, not a private philosophy. This gospel, and not the speculations threatening Colossae, is what actually produces faith, love, and hope.

epignosis.gloss: Full or thorough knowledge, deeper than mere information; personal, relational understanding. Against a heresy that promised hidden higher knowledge, Paul prays for a knowledge of God's will that is real and complete in Christ. This true knowledge is not abstract but leads to a worthy walk and fruitful obedience.

apolutrosis.gloss: Redemption, release secured by the payment of a ransom, echoing the freeing of slaves and the exodus. In the beloved Son we have redemption, defined here as the forgiveness of sins. The rescue from the dominion of darkness is not vague; it is purchased release into the kingdom of Christ.

basic.context: Paul writes to a church he has never visited, in the town of Colossae, founded through his coworker Epaphras. Before he confronts the false teaching that is unsettling them, he gives thanks for their faith, love, and hope, and tells them how he prays for them. He wants them filled with the true knowledge of God, so they can live lives that please the Lord who has already rescued them from darkness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real letter to a real congregation, and its opening thanksgiving and prayer set up everything that follows. Paul is not just being polite; he is naming the gospel that saved them and the growth he longs to see, so that when he defends the greatness of Christ, they already know they belong to Him. The prayer shows us what to ask God for in one another.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul writes as an apostolos, a sent one commissioned by God. He is thankful for their grip on the euangelion, the good news that is bearing fruit worldwide. He prays they would be filled with epignosis, real and full knowledge of God's will, over against a heresy promising secret knowledge. And he rejoices that in the Son they have apolutrosis, redemption, the forgiveness of sins.
basic.typology: Being rescued from the dominion of darkness and brought into a kingdom echoes the exodus, when God redeemed Israel out of slavery into His own care. Sharing in the inheritance of the saints in the light recalls Israel receiving its inheritance in the land. Christ is the greater deliverer, and His people the true inheritors.
basic.christ: Everything here flows to and through Christ. Faith is in Christ Jesus, hope is stored up in heaven, and rescue means being brought into the kingdom of God's beloved Son. In Him, and in no one else, we have redemption and the forgiveness of sins. Even before the great hymn of chapter one, Christ is already the center.
basic.god: God is the Father who is thanked, prayed to, and praised. He fills His people with knowledge, strengthens them with His glorious might, qualifies them for the inheritance, rescues them from darkness, and gives them His beloved Son. Salvation is His initiative from start to finish.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 1 opens with a similar thanksgiving and prayer for knowledge and strength. Philippians 1 shows Paul thanking God and praying for a church's growth in love and discernment. And Acts 26 records Christ sending Paul to turn people from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God.
basic.oneStory: This opening plants the letter in the big story: a people once in darkness are redeemed and brought into the kingdom of God's Son, just as Israel was brought out of Egypt into the inheritance. The gospel that began in Colossae is spreading through the whole world, carrying the hope of glory to the nations.

context.literary: A standard Pauline opening, greeting then thanksgiving then prayer, but every element is loaded with the letter's coming themes: knowledge, fruitfulness, growth, the supremacy of the Son, and redemption. The triad of faith, love, and hope in verses 4 and 5 frames the thanksgiving, and the prayer of verses 9 to 14 climbs to the great rescue statement that leads straight into the Christ hymn.
context.historical: Colossae was a declining town in the Lycus Valley of Asia Minor, near Laodicea and Hierapolis. Paul had not planted the church; Epaphras, a native of the region, had. A syncretistic teaching blending Jewish practice, ascetic rules, angelic speculation, and claims to secret knowledge was pressuring the young believers, which explains Paul's stress on the true knowledge found in Christ.
context.canonical: Colossians belongs with the prison epistles and is closely parallel to Ephesians. Its opening sets a pattern of gospel gratitude that runs across Paul's letters, and its language of rescue, kingdom, inheritance, and forgiveness ties it to the exodus story and to the whole biblical hope of a redeemed people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure an unvisited, pressured church of Paul's genuine thanksgiving and constant prayer, to hold up the gospel they received as the true and fruitful word, and to lift their eyes to the God who rescued them, so that they are anchored before the false teaching is answered.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's report of his own thanksgiving and prayer describes his apostolic practice, but it also models what believers should give thanks for and pray toward in one another: faith, love, hope, knowledge of God's will, a worthy walk, and endurance. The saving acts of verses 13 and 14 are declared truths for all who are in Christ.

debates.question: Is the hope in verse 5 a subjective attitude or an objective reality stored up in heaven?

views.label: Objective, the content hoped for
views.case: Paul says the hope is stored up for you in heaven and is the source from which faith and love proceed, so it is the secured inheritance itself, not merely a feeling.

views.label: Subjective, the attitude of hoping
views.case: Some read hope as the confident expectation in the believer; yet even then it rests on and points to the objective reality kept safe in heaven.

debates.bearing: Either way, Christian hope is anchored outside us in what God has secured, which is why it can steady a church under pressure and fuel present faith and love.

study.originalLanguages: Paul introduces himself as an apostolos, a sent one, commissioned by the will of God, so his letter carries Christ's own authority to a church he has never seen. His thanksgiving centers on the euangelion, the good news that is not static but bearing fruit and growing across the whole world, the same living word that took root in Colossae. His prayer asks God to fill them with epignosis, full and personal knowledge of God's will, a pointed word against a teaching that dangled secret knowledge before them. The section climbs to redemption, apolutrosis, ransomed release defined precisely as the forgiveness of sins, granted in the beloved Son into whose kingdom they have been transferred out of the dominion of darkness.

typology.type: Rescue from darkness into a kingdom
typology.fulfillment: Fulfils the exodus pattern, where God redeemed Israel out of Egyptian slavery into His covenant care, now realized as believers are transferred into the kingdom of God's beloved Son.

typology.type: The inheritance of the saints in the light
typology.fulfillment: Echoes Israel's allotted inheritance in the promised land, fulfilled in the imperishable inheritance shared by all who are in Christ.

study.christ: Though the great Christ hymn is still a few verses away, Christ is already everywhere in this opening. Faith is placed in Christ Jesus, the hope is kept in heaven, and the decisive act of God is to bring His people into the kingdom of His beloved Son. Verse 14 concentrates it: in Him, and only in Him, we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins. The whole rescue from darkness terminates in a person, the Son whom the Father loves, preparing the reader to hear that this same Son is the image of the invisible God and the one in whom all things hold together.
study.god: God is named first as our Father and the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. He is the one Paul thanks, the one he asks to fill the Colossians with knowledge and strengthen them with His glorious might, and the one who qualifies them for the inheritance. Supremely, God is the rescuer who delivers from the dominion of darkness and gives His beloved Son and redemption in Him. Salvation is entirely His gracious initiative, received with thanksgiving.

crossReferences.note: A closely parallel thanksgiving and prayer that the church would know God's will, hope, and power.
Paul thanks God for a church and prays their love would abound in knowledge and discernment.
Christ sends Paul to turn people from darkness to light, echoing the rescue of verse 13.
An imperishable inheritance kept in heaven, matching the hope stored up for you in heaven.

study.oneStory: This thanksgiving sets the letter inside the sweep of Scripture: people once under the dominion of darkness are redeemed and carried into the kingdom of God's beloved Son, just as Israel was brought out of Egypt into its inheritance. The gospel that bore fruit in Colossae is the same word spreading through all the world, gathering the nations into the hope of glory. From the first paragraph the story runs toward Christ, in whom there is redemption and the forgiveness of sins.

quiz.q: What three things does Paul give thanks for in the Colossians?

options.text: Their faith in Christ, love for the saints, and hope stored up in heaven.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 name the classic triad of faith, love, and hope.

options.text: Their wealth, influence, and social standing.
options.note: Paul gives thanks for spiritual fruit, not worldly advantage.

options.text: Their secret knowledge and visionary experiences.
options.note: That is the false teaching Paul opposes, not what he commends.

options.text: Nothing yet, since he had never met them.
options.note: Paul thanks God for them constantly, having heard of their faith.

quiz.q: What does Paul say the gospel is doing all over the world?

options.text: Bearing fruit and growing, just as it has among the Colossians.
options.note: Verse 6 pictures the gospel as a living, spreading, fruitful word.

options.text: Fading away as new philosophies replace it.
options.note: The opposite; Paul stresses its growth, not its decline.

options.text: Remaining hidden to all but a spiritual elite.
options.note: The gospel has come openly to them and to the whole world.

options.text: Producing division and confusion everywhere.
options.note: It bears the fruit of faith, love, and hope, not confusion.

quiz.q: What is the main thing Paul prays for the Colossians?

options.text: That they be filled with the knowledge of God's will in all spiritual wisdom and understanding.
options.note: Verse 9 is the heart of the prayer, leading to a worthy walk.

options.text: That they gain wealth and comfort.
options.note: Paul prays for knowledge, endurance, and fruitfulness, not comfort.

options.text: That they escape all hardship immediately.
options.note: He prays for endurance and patience within hardship, not escape from it.

options.text: That they keep the teaching secret.
options.note: The gospel is proclaimed openly and spreads; nothing is kept secret.

quiz.q: According to verses 13 and 14, what has God done for believers?

options.text: Rescued them from darkness into the kingdom of His Son, in whom they have redemption and forgiveness.
options.note: These verses summarize the great rescue and its ground in Christ.

options.text: Left them to rescue themselves by effort.
options.note: The rescue is God's act; He has rescued and brought them in.

options.text: Given them a set of rules to earn acceptance.
options.note: Redemption is by Christ, not by rule-keeping, as chapter two argues.

options.text: Promised a rescue that has not yet begun.
options.note: The verbs are past: He has rescued and brought them in already.

quiz.q: Why does the true knowledge of God's will matter, according to verse 10?

options.text: So that believers walk worthy of the Lord, please Him, bear fruit, and grow.
options.note: Knowledge here is not abstract; it produces a worthy, fruitful life.

options.text: So that believers can win arguments and feel superior.
options.note: The aim is a pleasing walk, not pride or debate.

options.text: So that believers can unlock hidden spiritual powers.
options.note: That echoes the false teaching, not Paul's purpose.

options.text: It does not matter much; only feelings count.
options.note: Paul prays earnestly for this knowledge as the root of godly living.

questions: Paul gives thanks for the Colossians before he corrects them. Who are the believers you could thank God for by name this week, and what fruit do you see in them?
Paul prays for knowledge that leads to a worthy walk, not just information. Where is God calling you to turn what you know about His will into how you actually live?
The gospel is described as bearing fruit and growing worldwide. Where have you seen it bearing fruit in your own life, and where do you long to see more growth?
God has rescued you from the dominion of darkness into the kingdom of His Son. How would living as a rescued citizen of that kingdom change how you face this week?
Christian hope is stored up safely in heaven, outside our circumstances. When life feels shaky, how does an anchored hope steady your faith and love right now?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts read and the Lord Jesus Christ after God our Father; the BSB follows the shorter reading, which does not change the sense.
The BSB ends this verse with an em dash before the following clause; here it is rendered with a comma to keep the flow, with no change to the wording.
Who has qualified you can also be rendered qualified us in some manuscripts; either way the Father is the one who makes His people fit for the inheritance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Colossians 1:1-14. The em dash at the end of verse 4 in the BSB has been rendered as a comma to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
